name: Florida
description: The Sunshine State — beaches, theme parks, the Everglades, and the tip of the lower-48.

questions.q: What is Florida's well-known nickname?

source.notes: Adopted as the official state nickname in 1970.

questions.q: What is the capital of Florida?

source.notes: Chosen in 1824 as a midpoint between St. Augustine and Pensacola.

questions.q: Which city in central Florida is home to Walt Disney World, the world's largest theme-park resort?

source.notes: Opened 1971; four theme parks and two water parks.

questions.q: Florida's southern tip is a famous chain of islands connected to the mainland by U.S. Route 1. They are called what?

source.notes: Tip: Key West, 90 miles from Cuba.

questions.q: What vast subtropical wetland covers much of southern Florida?

source.notes: UNESCO World Heritage Site.

questions.q: Which NASA launch site on Florida's Atlantic coast has launched astronauts since the 1960s?

source.notes: Adjacent Cape Canaveral Space Force Station.

questions.q: Which ocean borders Florida to the east?

source.notes: The Gulf of Mexico borders the state to the west.

questions.q: The Spanish explorer who 'discovered' Florida and named it in 1513 was whom?

source.notes: Reportedly arrived during the Easter (Pascua Florida) season.

questions.q: What does the Spanish name 'La Florida' translate to in English?

source.notes: Named for the floral season and Pascua Florida.

questions.q: Which city, founded in 1565, is the oldest continuously inhabited European-established settlement in the continental U.S.?

source.notes: Founded by Spanish admiral Pedro Menéndez de Avilés.

questions.q: Which large lake in south-central Florida is the 10th-largest natural lake in the U.S.?

source.notes: About 730 square miles.

questions.q: Florida became the 27th U.S. state in which year?

source.notes: March 3, 1845.

questions.q: Florida's largest city by population (within city limits) is which?

source.notes: Jacksonville is also the largest U.S. city by land area in the lower 48.

questions.q: Which Native American tribe resisted U.S. relocation efforts in three 19th-century wars bearing its name?

source.notes: Three Seminole Wars: 1817-18, 1835-42, 1855-58.

questions.q: Which Florida-based developer built the Florida East Coast Railway to Key West in the early 1900s?

source.notes: Flagler also developed Palm Beach and Miami.

questions.q: What is the official Florida state marine mammal, known for slow movement in warm coastal waters?

source.original_a: Rivers and coastal regions of South Florida
source.notes: Manatees are descended from elephants.

questions.q: The Castillo de San Marcos, the oldest masonry fort in the continental U.S., is located in which Florida city?

source.notes: Construction began 1672; built of coquina.

questions.q: Which political/geographic region is often used to refer to the conservative rural-northern strip of Florida?

source.notes: The Florida Panhandle is the northwestern strip bordering Alabama and Georgia.

questions.q: Which U.S. state has the second-longest coastline (after Alaska)?

source.notes: audit-corrected 2026-05-09: original premise was false — Florida is actually 2nd (after Alaska) by NOAA shoreline (~8,436 mi); Hawaii is not 2nd. Reframed to ask about 2nd-longest.

questions.q: Which 1821 treaty transferred Florida from Spain to the United States?

source.notes: Signed 1819, ratified 1821.

questions.q: Which 1992 Category 5 hurricane remains one of the most destructive to strike Florida?

source.notes: Devastated Homestead in August 1992.

questions.q: Which NFL team, based near Tampa, won its first Super Bowl in 2003?

source.notes: Super Bowl XXXVII vs Oakland; Bucs also won 2021.
